metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the lock-blob wire format (e.g., UTF-8 JSON, msgpack, or a named custom struct) so encode/decode and the corrupt-blob detection strategy are unambiguous.
findings.finding: The lock-blob serialization format is never specified. The plan lists four fields (holder, lease_secs, hlc_heartbeat, fence) and says 'encode/decode + corrupt-blob fail-closed path', but does not say whether the wire format is JSON, msgpack, CBOR, a custom binary layout, or something else. An executor must make an undirected design choice that affects the corrupt-blob detection logic and interoperability with any future reader.
findings.location: ## What — Lock-blob schema bullet; AC line 2
findings.suggested_fix: Add one line to the schema bullet: "Serialized as UTF-8 JSON; a decode that raises `json.JSONDecodeError` or produces missing/wrong-typed keys is treated as corrupt (fail-closed)."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `_is_cas_mismatch` (which hard-codes `refs/heads/tickets`) will be adapted for `refs/reconciler/*` ref names — either by parameterising it to accept a ref-name argument or by introducing a `_ref_lock`-specific variant — and confirm that acquire CAS (old=zero, exit-128 = 'lock held') is NOT retried while release CAS exit-128 IS retried.
findings.finding: `_is_cas_mismatch` in `_advisory_lock.py` hard-codes `'refs/heads/tickets' in args` as the CAS-race discriminator; if `_ref_lock.py` reuses `_cas_advance_with_retry`, any CAS exit-128 on `refs/reconciler/lock` or `refs/reconciler/gate` will NOT be recognised as a benign race and will propagate immediately as a fatal error instead of being retried — silently breaking the release-CAS retry path.
findings.location: AC line 3 / `_advisory_lock.py:415-426` (`_is_cas_mismatch`)
findings.suggested_fix: Refactor `_is_cas_mismatch(exc)` → `_is_cas_mismatch(exc, ref_name)` so callers pass the specific ref being operated on; have `_cas_advance_with_retry` (or a new `_ref_cas_with_retry`) thread the ref name through. Keep acquire-CAS outside the retry wrapper entirely (exit-128 = definitive 'lock held', not a race).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit note (in the plan or as a `concurrency.md` update AC) that the bare-ref lock supersedes the I6 file-lock exception and explain why the new approach is architecturally preferred over the existing file-based pattern — the plan currently introduces a second cross-client lock pattern with no justification relative to the established one.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a bare-ref CAS lock as a new architectural pattern replacing the file-based advisory lock (`.reconciler-pass-lock`), but `concurrency.md` I6 explicitly labels the file-based lock as 'the one allowed exception — not a precedent for new cross-client locks.' The plan does not acknowledge I6 or explain how the new ref-lock pattern satisfies or supersedes the I6 invariant, leaving an unresolved tension between the new architecture and the documented concurrency doctrine.
findings.location: What section: architecture overview; concurrency.md I6

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (with a codebase experiment or git documentation citation) that `git push`/`git fetch` of a `refs/reconciler/*` ref correctly transfers the pointed-at blob object to the remote — this is the load-bearing capability assertion for the whole primitive and must be confirmed before the architecture commits to ref→blob.
findings.finding: The plan proposes storing the lock as a bare git ref pointing directly at a blob object (`git update-ref <ref> <blob-sha> 0000...`). This is a non-canonical ref type — git normally expects refs to point at commits or annotated tags. The plan makes no assertion about whether `git fetch`/`git push` correctly propagates a ref→blob across remotes, and prior review findings (cached at `.rebar/cache/plan-review/6c9c…/3f8eaaf9…`) explicitly flagged this capability as unverified.
findings.location: What section: 'The lock is a git ref -> blob'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before wiring `_cas_advance_with_retry` for `refs/reconciler/*`, confirm whether `_is_cas_mismatch` needs to be generalized (e.g. accept a ref-name parameter or match any `update-ref` exit-128) so the reuse is actually correct — cite how this will be handled in the plan.
findings.finding: `_is_cas_mismatch` in `_advisory_lock.py` hardcodes `'refs/heads/tickets' in args` as part of its CAS-mismatch discriminator, so the `_cas_advance_with_retry` helper the plan directs implementors to reuse will NOT correctly classify exit-128 from `git update-ref refs/reconciler/lock …` as a retryable CAS race — it will propagate as a hard error instead. The plan's 'reuse' instruction is broken as-is and risks an implementor silently adding a parallel discriminator (the NIH outcome the plan was trying to avoid).
findings.location: Plan section 'What' bullet 3 ('Reuse the existing ref-CAS helper at `_advisory_lock.py:92-111`'); AC line 3
findings.suggested_fix: Generalize `_is_cas_mismatch` to accept an optional `ref_name` parameter (defaulting to `refs/heads/tickets` for backward compatibility), or broaden the check to `'update-ref' in args` without the ref-name guard, and update the plan's AC#3 to specify this. That way `_cas_advance_with_retry` works correctly for any ref, fulfilling the no-parallel-primitive intent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in the plan that `_is_cas_mismatch` is hardcoded to `refs/heads/tickets` and specify whether `_cas_advance_with_retry` will be generalized (e.g., by parameterizing the ref name check) or a new thin wrapper written — the plan's 'reuse, not a second primitive' AC must account for this constraint.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the existing CAS helper in `_advisory_lock.py` can be reused for arbitrary ref names (e.g., `refs/reconciler/lock`). However, `_is_cas_mismatch` (line 425) is hardcoded: `is_update_ref = "update-ref" in args and "refs/heads/tickets" in args`. Reusing `_cas_advance_with_retry` as-is for `refs/reconciler/lock` means a CAS mismatch on that ref will NOT be recognized as retryable — the retry logic will silently pass through as a hard error instead of backing off.
findings.location: Plan body (What section): 'Reuse the existing ref-CAS helper at `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_advisory_lock.py:92-111`' and AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `_is_cas_mismatch` (or the helper that wraps it) is ref-agnostic before committing to reuse; if not, specify whether the helper will be generalized (preferred, removing the `refs/heads/tickets` hard-code) or a thin wrapper is introduced — the current `refs/heads/tickets` guard will silently drop CAS-mismatch retries for `refs/reconciler/*`.
findings.finding: The plan directs reuse of `_cas_advance_with_retry` / `_is_cas_mismatch` from `_advisory_lock.py:415-426,447-487`, but `_is_cas_mismatch` hard-codes the check `'refs/heads/tickets' in args`, meaning it will never classify an `update-ref refs/reconciler/lock ...` failure as a retryable CAS mismatch — the retry loop becomes a no-op for the new ref.
findings.location: What section / AC#3: 'Reuse the existing ref-CAS helper at _advisory_lock.py:92-111'
findings.suggested_fix: Generalize `_is_cas_mismatch` to check for `update-ref` + exit 128 without constraining on the specific ref name (e.g., `is_update_ref = 'update-ref' in args`), and add a regression test covering both `refs/heads/tickets` and `refs/reconciler/lock` to guard the contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether 'reuse `_advisory_lock.py:92-111`' means (a) import only the budget constants, requiring a new ref-agnostic retry loop in `_ref_lock.py`, or (b) refactor `_is_cas_mismatch` to accept a ref-name parameter so `_cas_advance_with_retry` is genuinely reusable across arbitrary refs.
findings.finding: `_is_cas_mismatch` (the function that makes `_cas_advance_with_retry` retryable) hardcodes `'refs/heads/tickets' in args` at line 425; a CAS exit-128 on `refs/reconciler/lock` would NOT match, causing a concurrent-acquire/release race to be surfaced as a hard non-retryable error instead of a retryable CAS collision.
findings.location: AC line 3: 'The CAS is implemented by reusing `_advisory_lock.py:92-111`, not a second primitive.' / `_advisory_lock.py:415-426`
findings.suggested_fix: Refactor `_is_cas_mismatch` to check only `'update-ref' in args and exc.returncode == 128` (removing the hardcoded `refs/heads/tickets` guard), or extract a `_cas_retry_with_budget(fn, ref_name)` helper that takes the ref name explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `_is_cas_mismatch` (which hard-codes `'refs/heads/tickets' in args`) will be adapted for `refs/reconciler/*` refs before reusing `_cas_advance_with_retry` — e.g. parameterize the ref-name check, add a wrapper predicate, or extract a ref-agnostic variant.
findings.finding: The plan directs reuse of `_cas_advance_with_retry` from `_advisory_lock.py`, but that function's CAS-mismatch discriminator `_is_cas_mismatch` (line 425) hard-codes `'refs/heads/tickets' in args`. For any `refs/reconciler/lock` or `refs/reconciler/gate` ref, this predicate always returns False — meaning every lock CAS mismatch is misclassified as a hard non-retryable error, silently breaking the retry protocol. The plan never specifies how to adapt this ref-name-specific decision rule.
findings.location: What section — 'Reuse the existing ref-CAS helper at _advisory_lock.py:92-111'; AC line 3
findings.suggested_fix: Extract a `_is_update_ref_cas_mismatch(exc, ref_name)` helper (or make `_cas_advance_with_retry` accept a `is_mismatch` predicate), then pass a predicate that checks for the specific reconciler-lock ref name. Update the AC to require this adaptation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief ADR or CLAUDE.md entry documenting the bare-ref CAS lock design decision: why a git ref (not DB/advisory-lock), the blob schema contract (`holder`, `lease_secs`, `hlc_heartbeat`, `fence`), and the create-only acquire / expected-oid release invariants.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural primitive (bare-ref CAS lock with a defined blob schema) that future children will build on, but no ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or design-doc update is planned to capture the decision.
findings.location: ## What — overall plan; no documentation artifact mentioned
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `docs/adr/NNN-ref-lock-primitive.md` (or a CLAUDE.md section under `_engine/rebar_reconciler`) capturing: (1) rationale for bare-ref over alternatives, (2) blob schema fields and their semantics, (3) why `lease_secs` is relative, (4) the CAS acquire/release contract future children must respect.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the blob-creation step (`git hash-object -w --stdin` or equivalent) that produces the OID before the `update-ref` CAS acquire call, and confirm it will be covered by the new module or an existing helper.
findings.finding: `git update-ref <ref> <new-blob-oid> 0000...` requires the blob OID to already exist in the object store, but the plan names no mechanism (e.g. `git hash-object -w`) to create it, and no existing helper in `_advisory_lock.py` or the reconciler package performs blob creation.
findings.location: What / bullet 2: 'Create-only CAS acquire: git update-ref <ref> <new-blob> 0000000000000000000000000000000000000000'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet under the acquire description: 'First write the encoded lock payload to a blob via `git hash-object -w --stdin` (or `git hash-object -w <file>`), capture the returned OID, then pass it as `<new-blob>` to `git update-ref`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how `_advisory_lock.py:92-111` is reused for acquire: specify whether the retry budget is set to 0/1 for the create-only path, or whether only the release (expected-oid) path reuses the helper, to remove the contradiction between 'retry budget' and 'fail immediately on existing ref'.
findings.finding: The 'What' section directs reuse of the `_CAS_RETRY_BUDGET` retry loop from `_advisory_lock.py:92-111`, yet also states 'acquire simply fails if the ref already exists' (no retry). These are contradictory: a retry-budget helper retries on CAS failure, but create-only acquire must fail-fast when the ref is present, not retry.
findings.location: What section (CAS acquire description) vs. AC3 and 'No lease-expiry/steal logic' paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: State explicitly that only the release path (expected-oid CAS with retry) reuses the `_CAS_RETRY_BUDGET` loop; the acquire path calls `git update-ref` with old=zeros exactly once (no retry) and immediately raises `LockTaken` on non-zero exit code.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the lock blob schema to explicitly designate which field is the acquisition-time anchor used to compute `deadline = anchor + lease_secs` (e.g. add `acquired_at_hlc` distinct from `hlc_heartbeat`, or document that `hlc_heartbeat` at initial acquire == acquisition time and is updated on renewal).
findings.finding: The lock blob schema stores `lease_secs` as a 'relative duration, NOT an absolute wall-clock deadline' but does not specify which field carries the acquisition-time anchor needed to compute the absolute expiry; `hlc_heartbeat` is listed but its role (acquisition timestamp vs. renewal heartbeat) is unstated — leaving the expiry/steal child without a well-defined time-zero.
findings.location: Plan section 'Lock-blob schema' / AC line 2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the type and source of `hlc_heartbeat` in the blob schema (e.g., 'a monotonic integer from `_hlc.now()`, stored as a JSON number representing logical ticks') so encode/decode and test fixtures are unambiguous.
findings.finding: The `hlc_heartbeat` field is listed in the blob schema but its type, unit, and production site are never defined. The plan does not say whether this is an integer nanosecond timestamp, a (wall, logical) tuple, a string, or which module/function the caller should invoke to obtain a value when calling `acquire`.
findings.location: ## What — Lock-blob schema bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the semantic conflict between 'acquire simply fails if the ref already exists' (AC#1/What) and reusing `_cas_advance_with_retry` which retries on CAS mismatch: specify whether `_ref_lock.acquire` bypasses the retry loop (single-shot CAS) or calls a stripped-down variant.
findings.finding: `_cas_advance_with_retry`'s retry semantics are semantically mismatched for a create-only (old=zeros) CAS acquire: the helper retries a closure on CAS mismatch, but for acquire-if-free, a CAS mismatch means the lock is already held — retrying immediately would loop until budget exhaustion rather than returning 'lock busy'. The plan does not address this behavioral conflict with the stated 'acquire simply fails if the ref already exists' requirement.
findings.location: Plan 'What' section: 'acquire simply fails if the ref already exists' vs. 'Reuse the existing ref-CAS helper'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly confirm (in this plan or a cross-reference to the next child) that the lease-expiry/steal child will provide the complete RETIRE path for abandoned refs, and add a note in `_ref_lock.py` that the acquire-fail-if-exists behavior is intentionally incomplete pending that child.
findings.finding: The RETIRE/cleanup lifecycle is incomplete within this child: if a lock-holder crashes before calling release, the ref persists indefinitely. The plan explicitly defers lease-expiry/steal to the next child but does not confirm that the next child will fully close the retirement path — leaving the shared ref potentially leaking across child boundaries.
findings.location: ## What — 'No lease-expiry/steal logic in this child'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add unit tests for `read` (ref present → returns decoded blob, ref absent → raises or returns None per API contract) and for corrupt/empty blob decoding (must raise, not return a free-lock sentinel) to satisfy AC lines 1 and 2.
findings.finding: The test list in AC line 4 covers acquire/release scenarios but omits (a) `read` — which is a first-class export named in AC line 1 — and (b) corrupt/empty blob decoding, which AC line 2 explicitly demands fails closed; neither appears in the unit-test enumeration, leaving those behaviors unvalidated by the plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — lines 4 and 2 vs. test enumeration in line 4
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the test list in AC line 4 with: 'read on an existing ref returns the decoded lock blob; read on a missing ref raises/returns None (define the contract); decode of a corrupt byte sequence raises LockBlobCorrupt (or equivalent) and does not return a falsy/empty value that would be treated as no lock present.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete in-session proving command for the reuse constraint (e.g., a `grep`/`ast` import check in CI, or a test that asserts `_ref_lock` imports from `_advisory_lock`) so the structural AC is mechanically verifiable.
findings.finding: The acceptance criterion 'The CAS is implemented by reusing _advisory_lock.py:92-111, not a second primitive' is a structural code-layout constraint with no automated proving command — it can only be confirmed by human code review, not by running the test suite. The plan lists 'Non-integration suite green' as its sole mechanized check, which does not enforce the reuse constraint.
findings.location: AC line 3; ## What — 'Reuse the existing ref-CAS helper' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the test suite: `assert _ref_lock._cas_update is _advisory_lock._cas_update` (or equivalent import alias check) so a parallel CAS implementation causes a test failure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add structured log/metric instrumentation to at least three events in `_ref_lock.py`: (a) acquire failure (ref already exists), (b) release failure (stale OID), and (c) corrupt-blob detection — so operators can observe and alert on lock health.
findings.finding: The plan adds new failure points (git subprocess CAS failures, corrupt-blob detection) with no planned instrumentation — no log, metric, or trace is specified for lock-acquire failures, lock-release failures, or corrupt-blob events.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — all ACs; ## What — lock-blob schema section
findings.suggested_fix: At each failure branch, emit a structured log entry (e.g., `logger.warning('ref_lock.acquire_failed', ref=ref_name, holder=existing_holder)`) and optionally increment a counter metric; wire corrupt-blob detection to a `logger.error` with the raw blob bytes truncated for debugging.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add unit test ACs for `read`: (a) read on an absent ref returns None/raises a defined sentinel; (b) read on a valid blob returns the decoded schema; (c) read on a corrupt blob fails closed (raises, consistent with the blob decode AC).
findings.finding: The `read` function is specified in the module API ('acquire/release/read') but no unit test AC covers it, leaving its contract (what it returns when the ref is absent vs. present, and its corrupt-blob behavior) unverified by the test plan.
findings.location: AC section — unit tests list; '## What' — 'exposing acquire / release / read'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the required change to `_advisory_lock.py` (e.g., promote `_cas_advance_with_retry` to a semipublic symbol, or extract a shared utility) needed to support the cross-module reuse in `_ref_lock.py`.
findings.finding: `_cas_advance_with_retry` is a private (underscore-prefixed) function in `_advisory_lock.py` not listed in any `__all__`. The plan directs `_ref_lock.py` (a sibling module) to reuse it, but does not enumerate the required change to `_advisory_lock.py` — either exporting the symbol or acknowledging the cross-module private import — as an edit target.
findings.location: Plan 'What' section: 'Reuse the existing ref-CAS helper'; AC#3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the idempotency contract for `release`: specify whether a release that fails because the ref is already gone (zero oid) is treated as success (idempotent) or error, and cover this case in unit tests.
findings.finding: Release is not idempotent under retry: if `release` is retried after a successful CAS (e.g., due to a transient RPC error returning before the caller sees the result), the second call presents the now-stale old-oid and will fail. The plan does not specify whether a double-release should be treated as success or error, leaving callers unable to safely retry.
findings.location: AC: 'release requires the correct old oid; a stale-oid release is rejected'
findings.suggested_fix: Treat a release CAS failure where the ref no longer exists (current oid = zero) as a success/no-op, since the desired post-condition (ref absent) is already satisfied; document this in the module docstring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how the `fence` counter is seeded and incremented on each acquire (e.g., 'caller supplies it; defaults to 0 for first acquire and caller increments for re-acquire').
findings.finding: The `fence` counter is described as 'monotonic' and 'diagnostic' but the plan does not say how it is seeded (zero on first acquire? drawn from a global counter?) or who increments it (the caller passes it in, or acquire derives it from the previous blob). An executor must make an undirected choice.
findings.location: ## What — Lock-blob schema bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `fence` counter's generation rule in the lock-blob schema description (e.g. 'caller-supplied sequence number', 'HLC timestamp cast to int', 'starts at 1 and is stored externally', or 'random nonce for correlation only').
findings.finding: The lock-blob schema includes a `fence` counter described as 'monotonic' and '(diagnostic)', but the plan never specifies how its value is generated or incremented at acquire time — e.g. read-current-blob-and-increment, derive from HLC, use a random nonce, or accept as a caller-supplied argument. This is an ambiguous counter-placement gap.
findings.location: What section — 'Lock-blob schema ... a monotonic `fence` counter (diagnostic)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct AC3 and the What section to reference the actual reusable callable — `_cas_advance_with_retry` at `_advisory_lock.py:447-487` — rather than the constant-block at lines 92-111.
findings.finding: The plan's assertion that the reusable CAS helper lives at `_advisory_lock.py:92-111` is wrong. Lines 92-111 contain only a comment block and constant declarations (`_CAS_RETRY_BUDGET = 8`, backoff constants). The actual reusable callable (`_cas_advance_with_retry`) is at lines 447-487. AC3 instructs implementers to reuse `_advisory_lock.py:92-111`, which is not a function — creating ambiguity about what should actually be reused.
findings.location: Plan body (What section) and AC3: 'Reuse the existing ref-CAS helper at `_advisory_lock.py:92-111`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '`_advisory_lock.py:92-111`' with '`_advisory_lock.py:_cas_advance_with_retry` (lines 447-487)' in both the What description and AC3.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the line-number citation in the 'What' section and AC#3: the reusable CAS helper is `_cas_advance_with_retry` at `_advisory_lock.py:447`, not lines 92–111 (which contain only constants).
findings.finding: The plan says 'Reuse the existing ref-CAS helper at `_advisory_lock.py:92-111` (`_CAS_RETRY_BUDGET` / the `update-ref <ref> <new> <old>` path)' but lines 92–111 contain only a comment block and three bare constant declarations. The actual callable that implements the retry loop is `_cas_advance_with_retry` at line 447. The plan's line-number citation is wrong.
findings.location: Plan 'What' section: 'Reuse the existing ref-CAS helper at `_advisory_lock.py:92-111`' / AC line: '`_advisory_lock.py:92-111`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '`_advisory_lock.py:92-111`' with '`_advisory_lock.py` — `_cas_advance_with_retry` (line ~447)' in both the What section and AC#3.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the AC to cite the actual functions to reuse by name: `_cas_advance_with_retry` (line 447) and `_is_cas_mismatch` (line 415) — not just the constants block at lines 92-111 — so the implementer knows what to import/extend rather than duplicate.
findings.finding: The AC citation 'reusing `_advisory_lock.py:92-111`' points at the constants block only (`_CAS_RETRY_BUDGET`, backoff scalars), not the actual callable logic (`_cas_advance_with_retry` at line 447, `_is_cas_mismatch` at line 415, `_cas_backoff_seconds` at line 429) that the plan intends the implementer to reuse; this mismatch will cause the implementer to extract only constants and write a parallel retry loop, violating the AC's anti-duplication intent.
findings.location: AC line 3: 'The CAS is implemented by reusing `_advisory_lock.py:92-111`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'reusing `_advisory_lock.py:92-111`' with 'reusing `_cas_advance_with_retry` and `_is_cas_mismatch` from `_advisory_lock.py` (with necessary ref-name parameterisation)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the use of `hlc_heartbeat` over `time.time_ns()` in the lock blob, or replace it with a plain wall-clock nanosecond timestamp — the existing HLC is designed for ticket-event ordering (requiring a `tracker` path and `ticket_id`) and does not naturally apply to a coordination lock blob.
findings.finding: The lock-blob schema includes an `hlc_heartbeat` field drawn from the existing HLC module (`rebar._store.hlc`). The HLC was designed exclusively for ordering ticket event files (causal Lamport clock); using it as a heartbeat timestamp in a coordination lock blob is a novel secondary application with no precedent in this codebase. The plan gives no justification for why the HLC is preferable to `time.time_ns()` here — the HLC adds no causal-ordering benefit for a lock blob that is never replayed by the ticket reducer.
findings.location: What section: 'Lock-blob schema … hlc_heartbeat'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the cited line range from `_advisory_lock.py:92-111` to `_advisory_lock.py:447-487` (the `_cas_advance_with_retry` function) in both the What section and AC line 3.
findings.finding: The plan's citation `_advisory_lock.py:92-111` as the location of the reusable CAS helper is wrong. Tool-verified: lines 92-111 contain only a comment block and constant declarations (`_CAS_RETRY_BUDGET = 8`, backoff constants). The actual callable `_cas_advance_with_retry` is at lines 447-487. An implementer following the plan literally finds no function at the cited location.
findings.location: What section — 'Reuse the existing ref-CAS helper at `_advisory_lock.py:92-111`'; AC line 3
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `_advisory_lock.py:92-111` with `_advisory_lock.py:447-487` (`_cas_advance_with_retry`) everywhere it appears in the plan.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
